cards.grammarNote: aujourd'hui = au + jour + d'hui (literally 'on the day of today'). It can open or close a sentence: Aujourd'hui je travaille. / Je travaille aujourd'hui.
demain matin = tomorrow morning, demain soir = tomorrow evening. 'À demain' = see you tomorrow.
hier = yesterday. hier soir = yesterday evening / last night. hier matin = yesterday morning.
maintenant usually goes at the start or end of a clause. 'Pour l'instant' also means 'for now / at the moment' with a sense of temporariness.
Days of the week are never capitalised in French: lundi, mardi, mercredi, jeudi, vendredi, samedi, dimanche. The week starts on Monday (lundi) in France.
le + day = habitual/recurring: le lundi = on Mondays (every week). Without 'le', the day refers to the specific coming day: lundi = this Monday.
ce matin (this morning), ce soir (this evening), cette nuit (tonight or last night — context determines which). 'Cette nuit' can refer to the upcoming night or the one just past.
toujours = always (also means 'still': il est toujours là = he is still there). jamais pairs with ne: je ne mange jamais de viande. In spoken French, ne is often dropped: je mange jamais de viande.
These adverbs follow the conjugated verb in standard word order: Je vais souvent au marché. Je mange parfois au restaurant. Il sort rarement le soir.

cards.front: Yesterday evening, I went to the cinema.
cards.back: Hier soir, je suis allé au cinéma.
cards.grammarNote: Time expressions go at the start or end of a sentence, never between subject and verb. Both 'Hier soir, je suis allé…' and 'Je suis allé au cinéma hier soir.' are correct.
